currentPassword.description:
Requiring the current password stops an attacker who has stolen an
access token (say, from a logged XHR) from locking the real owner out.

newPassword.description: nefield ensures the new password actually differs from the old one.

password.description:
No min= here. Rejecting a 3-character password at login with "must be at
least 8 characters" tells an attacker the password was too short to be
real, and annoys a legitimate user whose password predates the rule.
Login either matches or it does not.

Password bounds, and why they are what they are:

  min=8  — NIST SP 800-63B sets 8 as the floor for user-chosen secrets.
  max=72 — bcrypt hashes at most 72 BYTES. Go's implementation returns
           an error above that rather than silently truncating (some
           other languages truncate, which means "correcthorse..." and
           "correcthorse...extra" become the same password). Rejecting
           it here produces a clear message instead of a 500.

Deliberately absent: composition rules ("must contain an uppercase
letter and a symbol"). NIST advises against them. They push users toward
P@ssw0rd1 — predictable to a cracker, hard for a human — while adding
no meaningful entropy. Length is what matters.

token.description:
32 random bytes, base64url-unpadded, is always 43 characters. The bounds
reject an obviously malformed value before it reaches the database, but
they are not a security control: the token's 256 bits of entropy are.

expiresIn.description:
ExpiresIn is the access token's lifetime in seconds. Clients should
refresh slightly before this elapses, rather than waiting for a 401.

refreshToken.description:
RefreshToken is the ONLY time the raw token is ever transmitted. The
server stores only its SHA-256 hash and can never show it again.

tokenType.description:
TokenType is always "Bearer". Sent so the client can build the
Authorization header without hardcoding the scheme.

data.description: Data carries the payload on success, and is null on failure.

errors.description:
Errors carries per-field validation failures, and is null otherwise.
This is the only error detail ever echoed back verbatim.

message.description:
Message is human-readable and safe to display. It is not stable; do not
branch on it.

meta.description:
Meta is an escape hatch for endpoint-specific extras that do not deserve
a top-level field.

requestId.description:
RequestID ties this response to the server logs. When a user reports a
bug, this string finds the exact request.

success.description:
Success is the single field a client should branch on. It is redundant
with the HTTP status code on purpose: some proxies and SDKs swallow the
status, and a body that says success=false is impossible to misread.

timestamp.description:
Timestamp is the server's UTC clock at the moment of the response. Useful
for diagnosing client clock skew, which breaks token expiry checks.

response.Pagination.description: Pagination is present only on list endpoints.

roles.description:
Roles is null when the caller did not request them, and an array
otherwise. Never an empty array standing in for "not loaded".

BearerAuth.description: Send the access token as: Bearer <token>
BearerAuth.name: Authorization

info.description:
A production-ready Go backend: JWT authentication, RBAC, and PostgreSQL.
Every response uses the same envelope: success, message, data, errors,
pagination, meta, timestamp, requestId.
info.title: Backend API

post.description:
Returns an access token (short-lived, stateless) and a refresh token
(long-lived, revocable). An unknown email and a wrong password return
the identical error, in the same amount of time.

post.description:
Revokes one refresh token, ending that session. The access token remains
valid until it expires; the client must discard it.

Consumes the refresh token and issues a NEW one (rotation). Presenting an
already-consumed token revokes every session for that user, because it
means the token was copied.
This endpoint is intentionally unauthenticated: the access token has expired.

post.description:
Creates a user, grants the default `user` role, and returns the account.
The email is trimmed and lowercased before validation.

post.description:
Always returns 200, whether or not the address has an account and
whether or not it is already verified. Reporting the difference would
let an attacker enumerate your users.
Heavily rate limited, so it cannot be used as a mail cannon.

Redeems the single-use token from the verification email.
The token is consumed on success, so the link works exactly once.
An unknown, expired and already-used token all return the same error,
so a token guesser learns nothing from the response.

patch.description:
Changes the caller's own name. Email, roles and active status cannot be
changed here; those fields do not exist on the request type.

post.description:
Requires the current password. On success, EVERY refresh token for this
user is revoked, including the caller's own, so all devices must sign in again.

delete.description:
Soft-deletes the account: the row survives so foreign keys still resolve,
but it is invisible to every read, and the email becomes reusable.
Requires the `users:delete` permission. You cannot delete yourself.

post.description:
Idempotent. Requires the `roles:assign` permission.
The change takes effect for the target user on their next login or token refresh.
post.summary: Assign a role to a user

get.description:
Answers "is the process running?". Touches nothing external, deliberately:
a liveness probe that checks the database turns a database outage into a
container restart loop.

Answers "can this process serve a request?". Pings the database. A failure
should remove the instance from the load balancer, not restart it.
